The Dark Side of the Boom

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: US launches fresh Iran strikes; 10Y jumps 8bps, Brent +3% in its biggest one-day move since June 1
items.idea: The 10-year Treasury gained 8.2bps to 4.55% and Brent had its largest single-day rise since June 1 (+3.01%) after the US struck Iran again and revoked the oil waiver that had let exports resume. Market-based inflation expectations had priced disinflation (1yr swaps at 2.23%), but the NY Fed's own consumer survey shows households bracing for 3.67% inflation next year, the highest since 2023. Bloomberg's tanker-crossing count shows Hormuz traffic still far below normal even after the 'calm' period.
items.angle: The 144bp gap between market-priced inflation (2.23%) and consumer-expected inflation (3.67%) is the tradeable dislocation if strikes keep escalating — TIPS breakevens look too cheap relative to what households actually believe.
items.tweetA: 1yr inflation swaps: 2.23%. NY Fed's own consumer survey: 3.67% expected inflation, highest since 2023. That's a 144bp gap between what the market is pricing and what the people buying gas actually believe. Iran just handed the bond market a reason to side with consumers.
items.tweetB: Everyone declared the Hormuz premium dead last week — Bloomberg's own story-count on the Strait hit its low on Monday. Then the US struck again and revoked the oil waiver the same day. The 'transitory' bet on this war just got un-transitoried for the second time this year.

charts.desc: 10Y yield spike, Brent 1-day move, Bloomberg Hormuz tanker-crossing count (still depressed), NY Fed consumer inflation expectations chart (3.67%, highest since 2023).

items.source: Bloomberg Points of Return / John Authers
items.title: Momentum's worst unwind with no external trigger — SocGen says it's the LOSERS rallying, not the winners falling
items.idea: The S&P 500 Momentum ETF vs. equal-weight index has been 'brutal' with no Covid-style catalyst behind it. SocGen's Andrew Lapthorne shows previous losers rallying — not winners falling — is driving the pain, and it's hitting Japan and Europe too, not just the US. Samsung's record profit still triggered a 9% Seoul drop, pushing chip-stock P/E to the biggest discount to MSCI World on record (lowest since 2019).
items.angle: When losers rally faster than winners fall across three regions simultaneously, that's a global quant deleveraging event, not sector rotation on fundamentals.
items.tweetA: Samsung just posted record profit and dropped 9%. Its P/E is now at the biggest discount to MSCI World on record. If you think 'record earnings + record cheap' means buy, you're pricing a normal cycle. The market is pricing something else.
items.tweetB: This momentum unwind has no vaccine, no Covid, no external trigger — SocGen's Lapthorne says it's the LOSERS rallying, not the winners falling, in the US, Japan AND Europe simultaneously. That's not sector rotation, that's a global quant unwind.

charts.desc: S&P 500 Momentum vs Equal-Weight ETF chart (brutal reversal); SocGen global winners/losers basket chart across US/Japan/Europe.

items.title: Vance's anti-GDP crusade is European-left degrowth rhetoric with a MAGA coat of paint
items.idea: Noah Smith argues JD Vance borrowed the 'GDP is a bad proxy for wellbeing' argument from the European left's degrowth movement, but redirects it toward protectionism and immigration restriction instead of redistribution. Vance uses a Japan strawberry-quality anecdote and personal stories (childcare, opioid deaths) to argue economics filled the moral vacuum left by declining Christianity, concluding 'maybe economics is just fake.'
items.angle: This is the intellectual cover being pre-built for slower growth from tariffs and immigration restriction — watch whether this framing gets deployed harder if Q3/Q4 GDP prints disappoint.
items.tweetA: Vance's actual argument isn't that GDP is wrong. It's that GDP going down is now acceptable collateral damage for tariffs and immigration restriction. That's the European degrowth left's playbook, just relabeled for a different base.
items.tweetB: When a policymaker starts attacking the metric right before a policy is expected to hurt that metric, the metric isn't the real target. Watch the next two GDP prints — if they miss, this is the pre-built excuse.

items.title: Corporate credit issuance hit $110B in June, nearly double last year — even as equities chop sideways
items.idea: June corporate credit issuance was $110B, the strongest month since March and roughly double last year's pace, with H1 YTD issuance at $685B. The author frames this alongside record single-stock volatility against collapsing implied correlation as evidence of rotation, not risk-off — arguing capital is flooding into AI compute the way it flooded internet stocks in the '90s, mortgages in the 2000s, and crypto in 2021.
items.angle: If credit issuance is melting up while the index chops sideways, leverage is building specifically around AI infrastructure financing — worth tracking which names (Oracle, hyperscaler debt) are absorbing the new supply.
items.tweetA: $685B in corporate credit issued YTD, June alone nearly 2x last year's pace. Everyone's watching equity vol. The credit spigot is wide open and pointed at AI compute — that's the actual leverage building in this cycle, not margin debt.
items.tweetB: Internet stocks in the 90s, mortgages in the 2000s, crypto in 2021, AI compute now — same playbook, just a new asset class absorbing the flow. The tell isn't the price action, it's who's issuing the debt to fund it.

charts.desc: Slide decks referenced but not embedded in email body — worth opening the livestream deck link.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman's desk calls it an 'ugly session': Samsung -9%, DeepSeek chip news, SOXL -50% from a 10-day-old high
items.idea: Samsung fell 9% in Seoul on record profit but 'not enough,' DeepSeek announced plans to develop its own AI chips, and high-beta momentum traded down ~6% with SOXL now down roughly 50% from an all-time high made just 10 sessions ago. Levered semi ETF volumes ran 30-60% above 30-day averages (SOXL +35%, DRAM +60%, SMH +30%), but 283 S&P 500 names still finished green — breadth wasn't wiped out.
items.angle: A China chip-independence headline plus a Korean earnings 'miss vs. sky-high expectations' was enough to crater a 3x levered semi ETF 50% in 10 sessions — that's a positioning fragility signal, not a fundamentals call.
items.tweetA: SOXL is down ~50% from an all-time high it made 10 trading sessions ago. Not from a crash — from Samsung beating estimates by 'not enough' and a DeepSeek headline about building its own chips. That's what a crowded, levered trade looks like right before everyone asks why they own it.
items.tweetB: 283 S&P 500 names finished green the same day SOXL got cut in half from its highs. This isn't a market rolling over — it's one overcrowded, 3x-levered AI-chip trade taking an air pocket while the other 490 stocks shrug. Don't confuse position unwind with regime change.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (ETF volume comparisons, momentum basket 5-day chart).

items.title: Japanese bond yields rising again — the macro headwind nobody's centered on
items.idea: JGB yields continuing to rise are lifting US Treasury yields in sympathy, a fresh headwind for risk assets just as the market priced a dovish Fed (July hike odds down to 17.6%, first hike pushed to October on a soft 57K jobs print). The News Wall pairs this with a genuinely improving BTC ETF flow picture (+$46.6M Monday, a second straight positive day after weeks of outflows) — a fragile, not-yet-confirmed reversal.
items.angle: Yen carry-trade mechanics (rising JGB yields pressuring global term premium) are becoming a bigger risk to US risk assets than Fed policy itself — worth tracking JGB 10Y against US 10Y for the next leg.
items.tweetA: The Fed just got priced dovish — July hike odds down to 17.6%, first hike pushed to October. Doesn't matter if Japan keeps forcing global yields up from the other side. The carry trade doesn't care which central bank is easing if the funding currency is repricing.
items.tweetB: BTC ETFs just had their second straight day of inflows after weeks of bleeding — genuinely the first real green shoot. It's happening at the exact moment JGB yields are creating a fresh global headwind. Watch which force wins first.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (JGB yield chart, BTC ETF flow chart).

items.title: Roundhill Memory ETF down 25% from its June 22 all-time high in two weeks
items.idea: Sandisk fell 10.1% and Samsung 6.9% the same day, and the Roundhill DRAM/Memory ETF is down 25% from its June 22 all-time high. The News Wall frames this explicitly as capital rotating out of the memory/AI trade and into crypto: 'the AI-chip leadership that sidelined crypto all year still cracking.'
items.angle: If memory names — the actual physical bottleneck of the AI buildout — are cracking 25% in two weeks, that's a harder tell than semis broadly, since memory pricing is the one input every hyperscaler capex model depends on.
items.tweetA: DRAM ETF: -25% in two weeks from its June 22 high. Not the speculative edge of AI — the actual physical memory bottleneck everyone worried about six months ago. When the scarcity trade cracks 25% that fast, ask whether the scarcity thesis just got repriced or whether this is a liquidity flush.
items.tweetB: The rotation-into-crypto thesis and the memory selloff are the same trade unwinding two different ways. If AI capex ROI questions are real, they show up first in memory (the input), not in the model companies (the output). Watch DRAM spot prices, not just Nvidia.

items.title: Deutsche Börse's Clearstream just added XRP, Solana, Cardano custody — TradFi plumbing is now altcoin plumbing
items.idea: Clearstream, Deutsche Börse's post-trade settlement arm, expanded institutional crypto custody beyond BTC/ETH to XRP, Solana, Cardano and more. This lands alongside an SEC ETF framework shift evaluating applications on structure rather than collateral, with Bitwise projecting 100+ new crypto ETF launches in 2026 and 126+ ETP filings pending.
items.angle: Custody infrastructure decisions are the leading indicator for institutional altcoin allocation, months ahead of ETF approvals — this is the plumbing move that precedes the product move.
items.tweetA: Clearstream isn't a crypto company. It's Deutsche Börse's settlement arm — the plumbing that clears European bonds and equities. It just added Solana and Cardano custody. When the plumbing moves before the ETF approval, that's the actual signal, not the filing.
items.tweetB: 126+ crypto ETP filings pending, Bitwise calling for 100+ new launches in 2026, and now Clearstream custodies XRP/SOL/ADA. Nobody needed a headline event for this — infrastructure is quietly showing up ready before the demand does.

items.title: Fannie Mae now counts crypto as a mortgage asset — first crypto-backed Fannie mortgage already issued
items.idea: Following a late-June FHFA order, Better and Coinbase issued the first Fannie-backed crypto mortgage — crypto holdings can now count toward mortgage qualification the way stocks and bonds do.
items.angle: This quietly opens a large new demand channel for crypto holdings to function as underwriting collateral in the largest debt market in the world (US mortgages) — a structural adoption vector distinct from and larger than ETF flows.
items.tweetA: You can now get a Fannie Mae mortgage using your Bitcoin as a qualifying asset. Not a loan against BTC — BTC counted like a brokerage account for underwriting. That's crypto quietly entering the US mortgage system through the front door.
items.tweetB: Everyone watches ETF flows for crypto adoption. The bigger structural move might be GSEs treating BTC as bankable net worth. That's a slower-moving but much larger pool of capital than spot ETF demand.

items.title: ~1M investors lost $3.8B on the $TRUMP memecoin, down 98% from its high
items.idea: Nansen data cited in the News Wall shows roughly one million individual wallets lost a combined $3.8B on the $TRUMP memecoin, now down 98% to $1.69 from its peak.
items.angle: A sitting president's namesake memecoin wiping out $3.8B from a million retail wallets is a regulatory and reputational landmine that hasn't been priced into the broader 'crypto is going mainstream' narrative sitting right next to it in the same newsletter.
items.tweetA: $3.8B lost by roughly 1 million wallets on a coin bearing the sitting president's name, down 98%. This is the story that ends up in a Senate hearing, not a tweet thread. The CLARITY Act debate just got a very convenient talking point for the opposition.
items.tweetB: Everyone points to institutional crypto adoption — Clearstream, Fannie Mae — as the bull case. Meanwhile a million retail wallets just got wiped out on a meme with the president's face on it. Both stories are true about this market at the same time.

items.title: CDX.NA.HY is trading 20-35bps above cash high yield — the same pre-dislocation basis seen two quarters before 2007's real move
items.idea: Headline HY OAS looks calm at 274bps (July 3), but the synthetic CDX.NA.HY index has run persistently wider than cash bonds since mid-Q2 — a basis that historically opens when dealers can't source cash bonds to hedge fast enough. The author notes the 2007 CDX-cash basis blew out roughly two quarters before cash HY actually cracked. Separately, the HY/IG spread ratio has compressed from 4.4x (July 2025) to 3.65x (July 2026) — investors demanding less extra compensation for junk risk, a classic late-cycle complacency signal.
items.angle: If CDX keeps running wider than cash while the HY/IG ratio keeps compressing, that combination — wider basis plus tighter risk premium — is exactly the setup that preceded 2007, just showing up in microstructure months before the headline spread moves.
items.tweetA: HY OAS: 274bps, looks totally calm. CDX.NA.HY: running 20-35bps above the cash index it's supposed to track. In 2007 that exact basis opened up two quarters before credit actually cracked. The index is lying to you; the swaps aren't.
items.tweetB: HY-to-IG spread ratio just compressed from 4.4x to 3.65x in a year. That's the market saying junk bonds barely need to pay more than investment grade anymore. Complacency doesn't show up as a low spread — it shows up as a compressed RATIO, and almost nobody charts that.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (CDX vs cash HY OAS spread chart, HY/IG ratio 18-month chart).

items.title: 2-year Treasury liquidity falls off a cliff two auctions off-the-run — bid-ask spread triples
items.idea: NY Fed data show the on-the-run 2-year note trades at a 0.66bp effective bid-ask spread; the first off-the-run 2-year trades at 1.22bps (nearly double); the second off-the-run trades at 2.23bps (more than triple). Swap spreads are deeply negative across the curve (2Y: -13.8bps, 10Y: -43.2bps, 30Y: -75.8bps as of late April), reflecting dealer balance-sheet constraints rather than credit risk.
items.angle: Every credit spread in the market is quoted against a Treasury curve that's losing depth just off the most liquid point — plumbing risk that nobody prices until a stress event forces everyone into the same illiquid corner at once.
items.tweetA: The most liquid bond market on earth loses two-thirds of its depth by the SECOND off-the-run 2-year note. Every credit spread you look at is implicitly priced against a liquidity that only really exists in the single most recent auction.
items.tweetB: 30-year swap spreads at -75.8bps isn't a credit signal, it's a dealer balance-sheet signal — the cost of financing Treasuries via repo now exceeds the yield itself in relative terms. When financing frictions get this large, credit hedges get expensive first, cash bonds reprice second. We're in stage one.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (bid-ask decay curve by auction cycle, swap spread term structure).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (citing Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen)
items.title: Oil and equities trade 'all clear' while rates markets are still pricing a term-premium problem
items.idea: Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen frames six cross-asset fault lines where pricing contradicts itself: oil and inflation act like normalization is complete, rates markets are still charging for a durable policy/term-premium issue, and gold has already absorbed the real-rate squeeze while equities and credit haven't. The piece argues this doesn't require a recession to unwind — just earnings failing to keep validating current valuations at elevated real yields.
items.angle: Gold already re-priced for higher-for-longer real rates; equities and credit haven't. That gap is the trade — either gold is wrong, or equities/credit have a repricing coming that doesn't need a recession, just one soft earnings season.
items.tweetA: Gold already paid the price for higher real rates. Equities and credit are acting like nothing happened. One of those two markets is wrong, and it doesn't take a recession to find out which — just one earnings season that doesn't validate the multiple.
items.tweetB: Six asset classes, six different stories about the same economy right now: oil says normal, rates say structural problem, gold says real rates hurt, equities say nothing to see here. Markets aren't supposed to disagree with themselves this much for this long.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (paywalled past the intro; six-fault-line framework from Deutsche Bank).

items.title: Redbook same-store sales +11.5% YoY for the week ending July 4 — no consumer slowdown here
items.idea: The US Redbook Index shows same-store retail sales up 11.5% year-over-year for the week ending July 4, per Koyfin data shared by Daily Chartbook.
items.angle: An 11.5% YoY same-store sales print is hard to square with a 'consumer is cracking' narrative — check this against the soft 57K jobs print circulating elsewhere in today's mail for a real divergence.
items.tweetA: Redbook same-store sales: +11.5% YoY for the week of July 4th. The jobs report just printed a soft 57K. Either the consumer doesn't care about the labor market yet, or one of these two data series is about to be proven wrong.
items.tweetB: +11.5% YoY retail sales growth is not a soft-landing number, it's a no-landing number. If this holds through July, the market's 17.6% odds of a July hike are priced for the wrong economy.

charts.desc: Redbook YoY same-store sales line chart, week ending July 4.

items.title: Managed money oil shorts at a 3rd-highest reading in 15 years, right next to record crack spreads
items.idea: Per Warren Pies via Daily Chartbook, managed-money short positioning in oil sits above the 40th percentile and among the third-highest readings in 15 years, coinciding with record crack spreads — a setup the author calls 'a recipe for a solid bottom.'
items.angle: This positioning data predates last night's fresh Hormuz tanker attacks and oil bounce (WTI back above $72, Brent to $76) — the crowded-short setup was already primed for a squeeze right before the geopolitical trigger hit.
items.tweetA: Managed money oil shorts were sitting at a 15-year extreme BEFORE last night's fresh Hormuz attacks. This wasn't a surprise rally — it was a crowded short trade that got the geopolitical excuse it was waiting for.
items.tweetB: Record crack spreads plus near-record oil shorts is a contradiction the market usually resolves violently. Refiners are printing money, speculators are betting on lower crude, and tankers are getting hit again. Pick a side, because the position isn't sustainable.

charts.desc: Managed money short positioning percentile vs crack spread level, 15-year lookback.

items.title: S&P put/call skew hits its lowest reading on record — crash protection is essentially free and nobody wants it
items.idea: The S&P put/call skew measure collapsed to 0.71, the lowest reading on record per Goldman Sachs data shared by Thierry Borgeat — the premium investors pay for crash protection relative to upside bets has essentially vanished.
items.angle: Skew at an all-time low landed the same week AI momentum cracked 20% in five sessions — a mispriced hedge, not a sign of safety.
items.tweetA: S&P put/call skew: 0.71, lowest ever recorded. Translation: paying for a crash is basically free right now. This printed in the same week SOXL fell 50% from a 10-day-old high. Somebody is mispricing tail risk, and it's probably not the person buying puts.
items.tweetB: Nobody wants crash insurance when insurance has never been this cheap. That sentence alone should make you nervous, not comfortable. Skew collapses like this either mark a real regime shift or the exact moment complacency peaks — check back in two weeks.

charts.desc: S&P put/call skew time series, lowest reading on record marked.

items.title: Schwab's retail trading index hits its highest level since February 2022 — 'no June swoon' among retail
items.idea: The Schwab Trading Activity Index (STAX) notched its highest reading since February 2022, with net-buying dominance sustained straight through what would normally be a seasonal June pullback.
items.angle: Retail is buying more aggressively than at any point in over four years, right alongside record-low put/call skew and near-record-low oil shorts unwinding — multiple positioning gauges converging on the same 'nobody's hedged' signal simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Schwab's retail trading index just hit a 4-year high. Same week skew collapsed to a record low. Retail isn't cautiously dipping toes back in — they're all-in exactly when professional hedgers stopped paying for protection. That combination has a name, and it isn't bullish.
items.tweetB: 'No June swoon' from Schwab clients. Translation: retail didn't blink during the AI momentum unwind. Whether that's conviction or denial gets tested by how this week's chip selloff resolves.

charts.desc: Schwab Trading Activity Index (STAX) history back to 2022.

items.title: Tech insiders are buying this dip — the people who know these companies best
items.idea: Per Jay Kaeppel via Daily Chartbook, insider-buying data shows at least one cohort of tech-company insiders treating the recent selloff as a buying opportunity, notable given insiders typically hold the best information on their own company's fundamentals.
items.angle: Insider buying diverging from the semi/memory unwind is worth cross-referencing against which specific names are seeing the buying — if it's semi/memory insiders specifically, that undercuts the 'AI capex ROI' bear case circulating elsewhere in today's mail.
items.tweetA: Tech insiders are buying into the same selloff that just cut SOXL in half. Insiders don't buy because a stock looks cheap on a chart — they buy because they know what's happening inside the building. Worth knowing which sector these insiders sit in before dismissing the AI unwind as fundamentals-driven.
items.tweetB: Every other signal today says complacency — skew at record lows, retail at a 4-year buying high. Insider buying is the one signal that's actually informed rather than sentiment-driven. Insiders buying plus retail euphoria isn't the same trade twice, it's two different trades wearing the same chart pattern.

charts.desc: Insider buy/sell ratio chart for tech names.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Hormuz becomes a 'toll road with IRGC snipers' — WTI back above $72 after fresh tanker strikes and waiver revocation
items.idea: Fresh attacks hit a Qatari LNG tanker, a Saudi crude tanker, and another vessel reportedly struck by drone in the Strait of Hormuz, pushing the maritime threat level to 'severe.' WTI pushed back above $72, Brent toward $76. The US separately revoked the June 21 Iranian oil waiver the same day — the economic anchor of the interim deal — tying Iran's sanctions relief explicitly to safe-passage guarantees. The author notes the oil market had gone heavily short on a 'surplus barrels return' narrative, leaving it exposed when the physical-risk narrative snapped back.
items.angle: This cross-references directly with today's Bloomberg piece and the Daily Chartbook oil-shorts chart — three separate sources converging on the same crowded-short, geopolitically-exposed oil trade from three angles (macro, positioning data, ground-level shipping risk) on the same day.
items.tweetA: A strait can stay 'open' on paper while becoming commercially dead. Insurers repricing risk and captains hesitating does the same damage as an actual closure, just slower and less visible on a map. That's the trade nobody's pricing correctly right now.
items.tweetB: The oil short was built on 'Iranian barrels come back online, surplus overwhelms the risk premium.' Then Washington revoked the waiver the SAME DAY tankers got hit. The bear case and the de-escalation case just died in the same news cycle, and the market was positioned for exactly the opposite.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Bloomberg ship-tracking data on Hormuz vessel traffic).

items.title: Nobody's modeling how HARD it'd be to build a Dyson Sphere — only how smart the AI would be
items.idea: Epoch AI argues AI-futurism debates (e.g., Ajeya Cotra's claim that superintelligent AI could develop near-light-speed spacecraft or nanotech within a year or two of automating AI research) focus entirely on how capable AIs will become, while almost nobody models how hard the target technology actually is to build. They propose a three-step research method: pick a concrete technology (e.g., self-replicating interstellar probes), assume specific AI capability levels (e.g., a 'drop-in remote worker' running on one H100), then estimate build time and resources required.
items.angle: This skepticism framework applies directly to AI-capex narratives — the same 'how capable will the AI be' question dominates every hyperscaler capex justification, while almost nobody separately models 'how hard is the physical bottleneck' (fabs, power, water, permitting).
items.tweetA: Epoch AI's critique of AI-futurism applies word for word to AI-capex bull cases: everyone models how smart the AI gets, nobody models how hard it actually is to build the power plants, fabs, and grid connections the AI needs. The intelligence explosion could be totally real and still bottlenecked by a two-year permitting process.
items.tweetB: 'Maybe economics is just fake' is JD Vance's line about GDP. 'Maybe the bottleneck is physical, not cognitive' is basically the same critique aimed at AI maximalism. Two unrelated newsletters, same underlying problem: we keep modeling the smart part and skipping the hard part.

items.title: Le Pen cleared to run in 2027 — with an ankle tag. France gets its first convicted candidate with a live shot at the Élysée
items.idea: The Paris Court of Appeal upheld Marine Le Pen's embezzlement conviction but shortened the electoral ban to one year of electronic monitoring instead of disqualification, clearing her to run in the April 18, 2027 first round while wearing an ankle tag. The roughly €474K embezzlement allegation (2009-2016) stands; she'll appeal to the Cour de cassation, which reviews procedure, not facts. National Rally is keeping Jordan Bardella publicly aligned as backup rather than activating a succession plan.
items.angle: This resolves the 'who leads National Rally into 2027' uncertainty markets have been discounting — a monitored-but-viable Le Pen keeps France's fiscal-conservative-vs-nationalist tension unresolved through the entire campaign, which is a more market-relevant outcome than either full disqualification or a clean Bardella-led ticket.
items.tweetA: France just created a new category: presidential candidate under monitoring-adjacent restriction. Every campaign stop is now a compliance event. French political risk should reprice not because Le Pen might lose, but because HOW she campaigns just got structurally weirder.
items.tweetB: National Rally didn't take the easy off-ramp of letting Bardella run clean. They chose to keep a convicted, tagged candidate as the brand. That's its own signal about who actually controls the French right, and it's not a legal calculation, it's a brand one.

items.title: Actuators are 56% of a humanoid robot's bill of materials, and China supplies 90% of the permanent magnets inside them
items.idea: In Tesla's Optimus Gen 2, actuators make up roughly 56% of total bill of materials across 28 units; Gen 3 adds 50 more actuators just for hand dexterity. Western manufacturers source 50-70% of actuator assemblies from Chinese suppliers (Ningbo Tuopu, Zhejiang Sanhua). China supplies about 90% of NdFeB permanent magnets, 40% of encoders/position sensors, and 40% of precision bearings used in humanoids. IDTechEx expects humanoid average selling price to fall from about $114,700 (2024) to around $37,000 by 2030; William Blair pegs current cost near $100K falling to $20-30K.
items.angle: The humanoid cost curve everyone models — ASP falling to roughly $30K by 2030 — implicitly assumes the China supply chain for rare-earth magnets and actuator assemblies stays open. That's the same chokepoint risk seen in EV magnets and wind turbines, not yet priced into humanoid equity narratives.
items.tweetA: 56% of a humanoid robot's cost is actuators. 90% of the magnets inside those actuators come from China. The 'humanoids will cost $30K by 2030' models are really 'China keeps exporting rare-earth magnets at scale' models wearing a robotics thesis.
items.tweetB: Everyone underwriting the humanoid trade is really underwriting the EV supply chain — actuators, magnets, and batteries are nearly identical components. That's bullish for cost curves via mature tooling, and bearish for anyone betting on near-term US-China decoupling. Pick one.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (McKinsey humanoid supply-chain component graphic).
